location.name: Aldeia do Muro
location.history: Aldeia do Muro — 'village of the wall' — clings to a basalt cliff face on the Portuguese Atlantic coast, its oldest houses built directly into the rock. The seawall itself is ancient, older than the village, and locals say it was laid by hands that predate Christianity on this shore. Fishing families have worked these waters for twenty generations; the catch is split, dried, and sold through a single cooperative that everyone depends on and no one fully trusts. The wall runs the length of the cliff-top, and children are told from infancy not to lean over it. Three people have gone over it in living memory. The sea here is cold even in summer.
location.weather: flat white haze, air thick with salt and no wind

crime.victim_state: dressed for a meeting — clean shirt, good shoes

characters.name: Abel Santos
characters.description: Abel Santos was the cooperative's bookkeeper — forty-three years old, precise, well-liked in the way that a useful man is well-liked. He had worked the accounts for eleven years without apparent complaint. The investigator finds him face-down on the rock shelf below the cliff wall, arms at his sides as though placed, wearing a clean white shirt and polished shoes. The back of his head is wrong. The tide has not reached him yet.

characters.name: Domingos Ferreira
characters.description: Domingos Ferreira is the cooperative's chairman — broad-shouldered, sixties, with the permanent squint of a man who spent his youth hauling nets. He runs the cooperative the way his father did: by feel and by favour. Abel Santos had been his bookkeeper, his chosen man. He will greet the investigator with correct concern and a firm handshake. He has been skimming cooperative funds for four years; Abel Santos found the discrepancy and arranged a private meeting at the wall to confront him before deciding whether to report it.

characters.name: Marta Vaz
characters.description: Marta Vaz mends nets in the shed below the cooperative and has done so since her husband drowned six years ago. She is quiet, observant, and generally believed to know more than she says. Abel Santos had recently refused to approve a loan she had applied for through the cooperative; she had not spoken to him since, and several neighbours noted she was angry about it.

characters.name: Padre Ernesto
characters.description: Padre Ernesto arrived in Aldeia do Muro eight months ago, replacing an elderly priest who died in his sleep. He is younger than the village expected, intense in his attentions, and has been seen walking the cliff wall at night 'for contemplation.' Abel Santos had been pressing him about irregularities in the church collection accounts, which the padre administered on behalf of the cooperative's charitable fund.

characters.name: Inês Drummond
characters.description: Inês Drummond is a cartographer from Lisbon, lodging with the widow Costa while she surveys the coastline for a government map commission. She is practical, direct, and carries expensive instruments. She and Abel Santos had been seen arguing at the cooperative office two days before his death, apparently over access to old survey records he controlled.

characters.name: Tomás Luz
characters.description: Tomás Luz is the harbour's young night-watchman — twenty-two, restless, ambitious enough to resent his position. He found Abel Santos on the rock shelf at first light when he walked the tide-line as routine. He is the one who fetched the investigator from the arriving boat. He had a debt with Abel Santos — a personal loan that Abel had recently called in.

items.name: Cooperative Ledger
items.description: A water-stained cloth-bound ledger, its spine cracked from repeated opening; several pages near the back have been torn out with some force, leaving ragged margins.

items.name: Broken Chair Leg
items.description: A turned oak chair leg, snapped near the base, with a dark stain along the wider end that has dried into the grain.

items.name: Abel's Unsent Letter
items.description: A folded sheet of paper in a coat pocket, addressed to the district cooperative authority in Lisbon, incomplete — it breaks off mid-sentence after the words 'irregularities I have confirmed in the accounts of'.

items.name: Silver Pocket Watch
items.description: A silver pocket watch engraved 'D.F.' on the case, the crystal face cracked, the hands stopped at eleven-eighteen.

items.name: Inês's Survey Notebook
items.description: A small oilskin notebook filled with grid coordinates and elevation marks; the last entry is dated the day before the death and notes a measurement taken from 'the old wall, eastern section, 22:40.'

locations.name: Harbour Steps
locations.flavour: Wide stone steps descend to the water, salt-bleached and slick; fishing boats knock against the pilings in the windless morning like something trying to get in.

locations.name: Rock Shelf
locations.flavour: A flat basalt shelf at the cliff's foot, exposed at low tide; the sea has polished it black and deposited rope, shells, and whatever the wall sends down.

locations.name: The Old Wall
locations.flavour: The ancient seawall runs the length of the cliff-top, chest-high and mortared with something that predates the village; at night the wind off the water sounds like argument.

locations.name: Cooperative Office
locations.flavour: A low whitewashed room above the harbour, smelling of fish-scale and ink, its shelves heavy with binders and its single window facing directly out to sea.

locations.name: Net Shed
locations.flavour: A long salt-dark shed where nets hang in loops from the rafters like the ribs of something enormous; the floor is permanently damp.

locations.name: Chapel of Santa Luzia
locations.flavour: A small, severe chapel at the cliff-top's landward end, its whitewash renewed every year; the saint inside has no eyes, which the priest says is correct.

locations.name: Widow Costa's House
locations.flavour: A two-storey house set back from the wall, its ground floor given over to dried fish and its upper floor rented to strangers when the money is needed.

locations.name: Drying Racks
locations.flavour: Rows of wooden frames strung with bacalhau stretch between the cooperative and the cliff edge, the salted fish stiff as boards in the white summer air.

locations.name: Tide Pool Path
locations.flavour: A narrow path cut into the cliff face, used by the fishers to reach the shelf below; in summer the rock retains the day's heat long after dark.

checkpoints.label: How did Abel Santos die?
answer_options: thrown from the wall — fall injuries

checkpoints.label: Where did the murder take place?
When did Abel Santos die?
answer_options: between midnight and two in the morning

checkpoints.label: What can be established about Abel Santos's state when he died?
answer_options: dressed for a meeting — clean shirt, good shoes
in working clothes — had come from the office

clues.text: Examining the body at [loc:rock_shelf], the investigator notes the wound at the back of the skull: a single deep depression, oval, with no stellate cracking — the kind made by something curved and heavy rather than an edge. The face and front of the clothing show no injury. Whatever struck him, it struck once.

The broken chair leg found at [loc:cliff_wall] carries a dark stain along its wider end consistent with the wound on the body below — the diameter matches, and there are two dark hairs caught in the grain. It was not thrown; it was left, half-tucked behind the wall's base stones.

[char:Tomás Luz] tells the investigator that when he found the body, the face and shirt were wet — 'soaked, like he'd been in the water.' He says the tide hadn't reached the shelf yet, and he doesn't know how to account for it.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The moisture on Abel's face and shirt came from the morning sea-haze condensing on the cold basalt; Tomás misread it as sea-water. The wound and the absence of water in the lungs rule out drowning, but Tomás's account plants the doubt.

clues.answer: thrown from the wall — fall injuries
clues.text: The investigator observes that the body lies thirty feet below [loc:cliff_wall], with no obvious route of descent other than the fall itself, and the rock shelf is the natural landing for anything going over the wall. The position — arms at the sides — reads at first as consistent with an impact fall.
clues.red_herring_explanation: A fall from that height would produce multiple fractures and likely facial injury; the body shows only the single posterior head wound. The position of the arms was arranged after death. The fall reading is initially compelling but does not survive scrutiny.

clues.text: Word reached the investigator that [char:Marta Vaz] had been heard telling [char:Tomás Luz] that Abel Santos's neck was marked — 'a bruise, a line, like rope.' Marta had stood close to the body before anyone moved it.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The mark Marta noticed was a crease from the shirt collar pressed into the skin during the hours the body lay face-down on cold stone — a post-mortem artefact. Marta's account is offered in good faith but is incorrect.

clues.text: At [loc:cliff_wall], the investigator finds a smear of dried blood on the inner face of the old stone, at the height of a seated man's head — and the broken chair leg tucked below it. The blood has run downward, not inward; whoever bled here fell away from the wall, toward the sea side.

The silver watch engraved 'D.F.' was found on the ground at [loc:cliff_wall], its face cracked, hands stopped at eleven-eighteen — the crystal broken on the wall's stone edge, not on the rock shelf below. It was dropped here, not carried down.

The cooperative ledger at [loc:cooperative_office] has had pages torn from the back — recent, judging by the clean fibres. The last entry in Abel Santos's handwriting is dated the day of his death, and ends mid-column. The candle on his desk had burned almost to nothing.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Abel was at the cooperative office that evening, working late — this is true. But he left the office alive; the candle burned down after he was gone. The torn pages were removed by Domingos before the investigator arrived, not as part of the murder itself.

clues.text: [char:Marta Vaz] tells the investigator, without being asked, that she heard voices near [loc:net_shed] after dark — 'two men, one of them angry, one low and trying to quiet him.' She cannot say if she dreamed it.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Marta likely heard a genuine argument — but it was earlier in the evening, between Domingos and a boat owner over a debt matter unrelated to the murder. The argument she heard did not occur at the net shed and did not involve Abel Santos.

clues.text: [char:Padre Ernesto] tells the investigator that Abel Santos had asked to speak with him privately at [loc:chapel_of_santa_luzia] 'about a matter of conscience' — and that Abel had never arrived for the appointment, which was set for eleven o'clock the night he died.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Abel Santos did plan to speak to the priest — he was considering whether to involve the church's moral authority before reporting Domingos. But he went to confront Domingos first, at the wall, and never made it to the chapel. The padre's account is truthful but misdirects toward his location.

clues.text: The cracked watch found at [loc:cliff_wall] stopped at eleven-eighteen — consistent with the moment of a struggle at that location. The tide table confirms the rock shelf below was dry until past midnight, meaning the body could not have arrived there earlier without tidal interference.

[char:Inês Drummond]'s survey notebook records measurements taken from the old wall's eastern section at [time:22:40] — she passed within thirty feet of the murder site less than an hour and a half before the likely time of death, and the wall was empty then.

[char:Tomás Luz] tells the investigator that he saw a figure at [loc:cliff_wall] just after the bell for eight o'clock — 'standing still, just standing, looking out.' He assumed it was the priest at his contemplation.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The figure Tomás saw was almost certainly Padre Ernesto, who does walk the wall most evenings. Abel Santos was still at his desk in the cooperative office at eight o'clock, as the ledger entry proves.

clues.answer: between midnight and two in the morning
clues.text: [char:Inês Drummond] tells the investigator that she woke in the night at [loc:widow_costa_house] to what she thought was 'something heavy being dragged' — she checked her watch: it was [time:half past one in the morning]. She went to the window but saw nothing.
clues.red_herring_explanation: What Inês heard was the widow Costa moving furniture in the room below, a habit the landlady has when she cannot sleep. It had nothing to do with Abel Santos.

clues.text: Examining the body on [loc:rock_shelf], the investigator notices the skin is cool but not cold — and in the summer heat the investigator considers whether the time of death might be more recent than first assumed, perhaps close to the discovery itself.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The rock shelf retains heat from the previous day's sun; the ambient temperature on the coast is mild overnight. The warmth of the body surface is environmental, not indicative of recent death. The watch and tide table data correct this misreading.

clues.answer: dressed for a meeting — clean shirt, good shoes
clues.text: The body at [loc:rock_shelf] is dressed in a pressed white shirt, good leather shoes, and a waistcoat — not working clothes. The shirt is clean of fish-smell and ink; Abel Santos changed before he went out that night.

clues.answer: dressed for a meeting — clean shirt, good shoes
clues.text: [char:Marta Vaz] tells the investigator that she saw [char:Abel Santos] walking past the [loc:net_shed] late in the evening — 'dressed up, like he was going to see the priest or someone important. Not like himself at that hour.'

clues.answer: in working clothes — had come from the office
clues.text: [char:Tomás Luz] tells the investigator at [loc:cooperative_office] that Abel Santos almost never left work without his coat, and the coat is still on the hook — 'he always wore it out, even in summer, it was his habit.'
clues.red_herring_explanation: Abel left without his coat because he intended only a brief conversation at the wall before returning. The missing coat suggests he left the office quickly, but Tomás's inference — that he never left at all — is incorrect.

clues.text: One cuff of Abel Santos's shirt is torn at the button — a small tear, easily missed. The investigator notes it and considers whether it preceded the killing.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The cuff was torn when the body was dragged slightly along the basalt shelf by the initial impact of landing. It is post-mortem damage, not evidence of a prior struggle.

clues.text: In the cooperative office, the investigator finds a travel bag beneath Abel Santos's desk, partly packed — a change of clothes, a money purse with coins, and a folded schedule for the coastal steamer.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Abel Santos had been planning a trip to Lisbon to deliver his findings about the cooperative accounts to the district authority in person — the unsent letter was a draft. He had not yet decided to go; the bag was preparatory, not imminent. He was not dressed for travel on the night he died.

clues.text: The watch engraved 'D.F.' places [char:Domingos Ferreira] at [loc:cliff_wall] at [time:eleven-eighteen] — the precise time it cracked against the stone during a struggle. When shown the watch, Domingos offers no explanation for how it came to be there.

Confronted at [loc:cooperative_office] with the ledger and its torn pages, [char:Domingos Ferreira] says Abel Santos must have removed them himself. But the remaining stubs show the tearing came from outside the binding — from someone reaching in, not someone turning pages.

[char:Tomás Luz] is the one who found the body and the first to reach the investigator — convenient, the investigator notes. He had a personal debt with Abel Santos that Abel had called in the week before. He is evasive when asked where he was between ten and midnight.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Tomás was evasive because he was with a married woman from the village during those hours — not because he killed Abel Santos. His motive is real, but he was not present at the wall.

clues.text: [char:Padre Ernesto] admits to the investigator that he walks the cliff wall [loc:cliff_wall] most nights, often past midnight, and that he had financial disagreements with Abel Santos over the charitable fund. He cannot prove he was in the chapel all evening.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The padre walks the wall habitually, which makes him a plausible suspect, and his financial dispute with Abel is real. But he was at the chapel until after eleven, as confirmed by the sacristan who locked up — and the murder occurred at eleven-eighteen.

clues.text: [char:Inês Drummond]'s notebook confirms she was at the wall [time:at twenty to eleven] — less than forty minutes before the murder. She and Abel Santos had argued publicly. Asked directly, she says she went straight back to [loc:widow_costa_house], but no one saw her arrive.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Inês did return directly to her lodgings; the widow Costa was awake and saw her come in just after eleven. The widow's confirmation was not obtained on the first pass of the investigation, making Inês appear unaccounted for.

clues.text: The unsent letter found on the body is addressed to the district cooperative authority and begins to name irregularities in the accounts — in [char:Domingos Ferreira]'s accounts specifically, though the name is not yet written. Abel Santos was not reporting a suspicion; he had confirmed it.

The torn pages in the cooperative ledger at [loc:cooperative_office] correspond precisely to the four years of [char:Domingos Ferreira]'s chairmanship — the period Abel Santos had flagged in his letter. What remains in the ledger is enough to show the gap; what was torn out would have confirmed it entirely.

[char:Tomás Luz] tells the investigator at [loc:harbour_steps] that Abel Santos had called in debts from three people in the month before he died — 'collecting, like he needed money fast or like he was settling things.' Tomás was one of them.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Abel was collecting debts because he was preparing to leave for Lisbon and wanted his affairs in order — not because someone owed him a fatal grudge. The debt collection points to Tomás's motive but is circumstantial and ultimately misdirects from Domingos.

clues.text: [char:Marta Vaz] tells the investigator at [loc:net_shed] that her husband's drowning — officially an accident — was made possible by Abel Santos's decision to approve an unseaworthy boat for the cooperative fleet. She has never forgiven him, and does not pretend otherwise.
clues.red_herring_explanation: Marta's grievance is real and her anger genuine, but she is not the killer. She provides this information unsolicited and directly — someone who had just committed murder would be less forthcoming about their motive.

clues.text: [char:Padre Ernesto] confides to the investigator that Abel Santos had become 'fixated' on the cooperative accounts — 'he spoke of little else in confession, the same numbers, the same suspicions, week after week.' He worried Abel was losing himself in it.
clues.red_herring_explanation: The padre's characterisation of Abel as obsessive is partly accurate — Abel was deeply focused on the discrepancy — but it implies the motive was internal, a fixation that provoked his own killing, rather than what it actually was: Domingos acting to silence a man who had proof.

leads.text: [char:Tomás Luz] stands at the top of [loc:harbour_steps] watching the investigator disembark, his hands working the hem of his jacket — he was the one who found the body, and he looks like a man who has not slept.
The body lies on [loc:rock_shelf] below the old wall, and the tide path up the cliff face is still wet with morning damp — the only direct route between the shelf and [loc:cliff_wall] above.

Smoke is rising from the [loc:cooperative_office] chimney despite the summer heat, and [char:Domingos Ferreira] can be seen through the window moving between the shelves — the chairman, the investigator is told, arrived there before anyone else this morning.

opening_narrative:
The coastal steamer from Porto arrived at [loc:harbour_steps] of Aldeia do Muro two hours after dawn, carrying two crates of salted pork, a cask of lamp oil, and the investigator. The village was already awake — fishers moved on the quay, nets were being carried, and the smell of the drying racks reached the water before the boat touched the steps. Nothing appeared wrong until [char:Tomás Luz] came down at a run.

Tomás was night-watchman, and he had been on the tide-line that morning as was his routine. He had found a man on [loc:rock_shelf], below the old wall. He did not say it was a body at first — he said it was [char:Abel Santos], as though the name explained the urgency. He had come straight to the arriving boat, he said, because he didn't know who else to go to.

The investigator was taken to the shelf by the tide path. The other villagers who had gathered on the cliff-top above were kept back. [char:Domingos Ferreira], the cooperative chairman, arrived at [loc:cooperative_office] while the investigator was still on the path down — he had been told by someone, and by the time the investigator returned from the shelf, smoke was already rising from the office chimney in the flat white morning air.
